summary: Gruber pushes back against Elizabeth Lopatto's argument that people should stop using generative AI for research, while fully acknowledging the real problem of AI-generated misinformation. He uses the example of Charles P. Pierce's retracted Esquire column — built on a fabricated presidential pardon — to illustrate the dangers of blind trust in AI outputs. However, Gruber argues that treating AI like an intern research assistant (useful but requiring verification) is the right framework. He draws a parallel to 1990s arguments against citing web pages, noting that new information technologies require adaptation, not rejection. He demonstrates his approach by prompting ChatGPT about controversial presidential pardons and getting largely accurate, useful results.
key_points: People are embarrassing themselves by blindly trusting generative AI answers, as illustrated by the retracted Esquire column about a nonexistent Bush pardon
Gruber's rule of thumb: use web search (Kagi) for finding articles/pages, use ChatGPT for answering questions — but verify everything
The right mental model for AI is a college intern research assistant: expect accuracy but double-check everything
Arguments against using generative AI for research mirror 1990s arguments against citing web pages — the technology isn't going away, so we must adapt
Generative AI won't replace web search, just as the web didn't replace libraries
money_quotes: I direct (and trust) ChatGPT as I would a college intern working as a research assistant. I expect accuracy, but assume that I need to double-check everything.
My basic rule of thumb is that if I'm looking for an article or web page, I use web search (Kagi); if I'm looking for an answer to a question, though, I use ChatGPT (4o).
But all of the arguments being made today against using generative AI to answer questions sound exactly like the arguments against citing web pages as sources in the 1990s.

But it's just a new technology — one that isn't going anywhere because it's incredibly useful in ways nothing else is, but its inherent downsides will force us to adapt and learn new ways of sourcing, citing, and verifying information.
The rise of the web didn't make libraries go away. Generative AI won't make web search go away.
If I had wanted to write a column about presidential pardons, I'd find ChatGPT's assistance a far better starting point than I'd have gotten through any general web search.
key_insight: Generative AI is genuinely useful for research when treated like an intern — trusted as a starting point but always verified — and dismissing it entirely repeats the same mistake people made about the web in the 1990s.
